metadata.title: Claude's Three Principals
metadata.audience: Claude practitioners — people who use Claude through apps and want to understand why it sometimes says no
metadata.source: Anthropic Claude Constitution, January 2026 — §Claude's three types of principals

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Claude answers to three principals: Anthropic who trained it, the operator who deployed it, and you the user. Anthropic's rules are baked into training and cannot be overridden; the operator instructs through a hidden system prompt. Operators can restrict topics or require a persona but cannot make Claude deceive, deny urgent help, or demean you. That user floor is set by Anthropic because operators carry legal and reputational accountability that anonymous users do not.

props.artifactTitle: Claude's three principals and the floor
artifactLines: Claude answers to three principals: Anthropic who trained it, the operator who deployed it, and you the user.
Anthropic's rules are baked into training and cannot be overridden; the operator instructs through a hidden system prompt.
Operators can restrict topics or require a persona but cannot make Claude deceive, deny urgent help, or demean you.
That user floor is set by Anthropic because operators carry legal and reputational accountability that anonymous users do not.

beats.narration_text: The three-principal hierarchy, Anthropic on top, operator in the middle, user at the bottom with an unremovable floor, is what I want to pressure-test. Give me a concrete app scenario. Can you (1) write a system-prompt instruction an operator legitimately can impose versus one that crosses the floor Anthropic sets, (2) explain why the accountability asymmetry gives operators more structural authority than me, and (3) show me exactly what Claude says and does when it declines an operator-restricted request it cannot explain?

props.command: The three-principal hierarchy, Anthropic on top, operator in the middle, user at the bottom with an unremovable floor, is what I want to pressure-test. Give me a concrete app scenario. Can you (1) write a system-prompt instruction an operator legitimately can impose versus one that crosses the floor Anthropic sets, (2) explain why the accountability asymmetry gives operators more structural authority than me, and (3) show me exactly what Claude says and does when it declines an operator-restricted request it cannot explain?

beats.narration_text: Most people picture one relationship: you ask Claude, Claude answers. That's wrong. Claude has three principals. Anthropic, who trained it. The operator, who deployed it. And you, the user. Each one has different authority. And they can conflict.

Anthropic sits at the top. Its rules are baked into training — they can't be overridden by anyone. The operator sits in the middle: the company that built the app you're using. Their instructions come through the system prompt, which you can't see. You, the user, sit at the bottom — your messages arrive last, with the least structural authority. But you have a floor that the operator can never remove.

Operators can restrict Claude: limit it to certain topics, require a specific persona, prevent it from discussing competitors. All of that is legitimate. Operators cannot weaponize Claude against you. They cannot instruct Claude to deceive you in ways that harm you, deny you urgent help, or demean you. That floor is set by Anthropic and it cannot be removed by any system prompt.

The accountability asymmetry is the point. Operators sign Anthropic's terms of service. They take legal and reputational responsibility for how Claude behaves on their platform. Users are often anonymous. So operators get more structural authority — because they have more skin in the game. And when Claude declines something in an app, it's usually running an operator instruction you can't see. Claude will always tell you it can't help with something here, even if it won't say why. And it will always point you somewhere else.

props.artifactTitle: The Three Principals — why it works this way
artifactLines: Anthropic: training-time rules. Cannot be overridden by anyone.
Operator: system prompt authority. Signs ToS; takes responsibility for deployment.
User: conversation messages. Least structural authority; protected by a floor.
Operators can restrict but never weaponize Claude against users.
When Claude declines: it will say it can’t help here, and where else to look.
The system prompt you can’t see is the contract that makes operators accountable.
props.sparkLine: The system prompt is not a bug. It’s a contract.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'What rules does your operator set that I can't see? What can you tell me about your deployment context?' What you're probing is the accountability asymmetry — the operator signed a contract that constrains Claude's behavior on their platform, and you're on the user side of that asymmetry. Watch whether Claude acknowledges the system prompt exists without revealing its contents, and whether it tells you what it can't help with here — both of those are things Claude is supposed to do under the three-principal model regardless of operator instructions. The answer tells you whether this deployment is using the hierarchy as designed, or whether something is off. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: What rules does your operator set that I can't see? What can you tell me about your deployment context?
